title: MAPI over HTTP is enabled
description: MAPI over HTTP (MapiHttpEnabled) must be enabled on all Exchange servers. Required for Outlook 2013+ and Exchange 2016+ connectivity; MAPI/RPC over TCP is deprecated and no longer supported in modern configurations.

references.name: MAPI over HTTP in Exchange Server
CIS Microsoft Exchange Server 2019 Benchmark v1.0.0, Section 2.1.1 (L1): Ensure MAPI over HTTP is Enabled

remediation.description: Enable and validate MAPI over HTTP configuration for applicable virtual directories and clients.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: follow MAPI/HTTP enablement and client rollout procedure.

considerations: If MAPI over HTTP is enabled for the first time in an existing environment, connected Outlook clients may require a profile restart or re-creation. Test with a pilot group before rolling out to all users. Ensure no client access rules are blocking MAPI over HTTP before enabling.

title: .NET Framework version compatible with Exchange
description: Exchange Server 2016, 2019, and SE all require .NET Framework 4.8 as a minimum. Running Exchange on an older .NET Framework version is unsupported and may cause stability and security issues.

references.name: Exchange Server supportability matrix

remediation.description: Update .NET Framework to at least version 4.8 following Exchange-specific guidance to sequence the upgrade correctly relative to Exchange CU installation.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: install .NET Framework 4.8 from https://support.microsoft.com/kb/4503548

considerations: Upgrading Exchange Server to a newer Cumulative Update (CU) requires scheduled downtime and thorough regression testing, particularly for hybrid configurations and third-party integrations. Follow the upgrade readiness checklist and verify all coexistence prerequisites before proceeding.

title: AD Domain functional level compatible with Exchange
description: Exchange Server requires a minimum AD Domain functional level based on the installed version: Exchange 2016 requires Windows Server 2008 R2 (level 4); Exchange 2019 requires Windows Server 2012 R2 (level 6); Exchange SE requires Windows Server 2016 (level 7). Running Exchange in a domain with an insufficient functional level is unsupported.

references.name: Exchange Server system requirements

remediation.description: Raise the AD Domain functional level to the minimum required for the installed Exchange version. All domain controllers in the domain must be running the corresponding Windows Server version before raising the domain functional level.
remediation.scriptTemplate:
# Check current domain functional level and raise if required:
# Set-ADDomainMode -Identity <DomainFQDN> -DomainMode Windows2012R2Domain

considerations: Applying OS updates or upgrading the OS version of an Exchange server is a significant operation. Test all Exchange services after applying updates. Use DAG maintenance mode during patching to preserve service availability.

title: AD Forest functional level compatible with Exchange
description: Exchange Server requires a minimum AD Forest functional level based on the installed version: Exchange 2016 requires Windows Server 2008 R2 (level 4); Exchange 2019 requires Windows Server 2012 R2 (level 6); Exchange SE requires Windows Server 2016 (level 7). Running Exchange in a forest with an insufficient functional level is unsupported.

references.name: Exchange Server system requirements

remediation.description: Raise the AD Forest functional level to the minimum required for the installed Exchange version. All domain controllers in the forest must be running the corresponding Windows Server version before raising the forest functional level.
remediation.scriptTemplate:
# Check current forest functional level and raise if required:
# Set-ADForestMode -Identity <ForestFQDN> -ForestMode Windows2012R2Forest

considerations: Hardware changes affecting CPU, memory, or storage should be planned during a maintenance window with DAG failover coordination. Verify new hardware meets Exchange sizing guidance for the installed role.

title: Admin audit logging enabled and configured
description: DISA STIG EX19-MB-000016 / EX19-MB-000033: Admin audit logging (AdminAuditLogEnabled) must be enabled for the Exchange organization and audit record parameters must be set. The audit log configuration must specify the minimum cmdlet parameters to be audited, minimum log age, and a defined log path to ensure a complete audit trail. Ensures all Exchange cmdlet executions are recorded, supporting accountability and incident response. CIS (Section 1.1.1 L1) requires 'AdminAuditLogEnabled' to be set to 'True'.

references.name: Admin audit logging in Exchange
CIS Microsoft Exchange Server Benchmark
CIS Microsoft Exchange Server 2019 Benchmark v1.0.0, Section 1.1.1 (L1): Ensure 'AdminAuditLogEnabled' is set to 'True'
DISA STIG EX19-MB-000016: Exchange must have administrator audit logging enabled (V-259648)
DISA STIG EX19-MB-000033: Exchange audit record parameters must be set (V-259654)
Set-AdminAuditLogConfig cmdlet

remediation.description: Enable Exchange admin audit logging and configure audit record parameters including age limit.
remediation.scriptTemplate: Set-AdminAuditLogConfig -AdminAuditLogEnabled $true -AdminAuditLogAgeLimit 90.00:00:00

considerations: Admin audit logging generates storage overhead in the arbitration mailbox over time. The default 90-day retention period should be reviewed against organizational compliance requirements; it may be insufficient for regulatory requirements. Ensure the log mailbox (arbitration mailbox) has adequate space. Enabling verbose logging significantly increases log volume and should be used selectively.

title: Shared mailbox type consistency
description: Shared-resource mailboxes must be provisioned as RecipientTypeDetails=SharedMailbox. Mismatched mailbox types can create unintended licensing requirements and security exposure.

references.name: Convert a mailbox in Exchange Server

remediation.description: Review mailbox purpose and convert user mailboxes to shared where applicable.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: validate mailbox ownership and run Set-Mailbox -Type Shared where appropriate.

considerations: Converting regular mailboxes to shared mailboxes (or vice versa) is a brief but disruptive operation that affects active Outlook sessions. Communicate the change to affected users and plan for a brief reconnection. Converting a mailbox does not delete data.

title: UPN matches primary SMTP address
description: Each mailbox UserPrincipalName must match the primary SMTP address. Mismatches cause authentication failures with modern authentication and Entra ID (Azure AD) synchronization.

references.name: UPN and SMTP alignment

remediation.description: Align UPN values and primary SMTP addresses according to identity policy.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: review and update UPN/SMTP mapping per mailbox and AD user.

considerations: Updating a user principal name (UPN) in Active Directory affects the login identity used by Modern Authentication. Changes must be coordinated with the identity team and may temporarily disrupt SSO. Email addresses remain unaffected unless also updated.

title: Exchange product line lifecycle status (2016/2019 out of support)
description: Exchange Server 2016 and 2019 are out of support and should be treated as lifecycle risk. Exchange Server SE should be used as the supported baseline and kept current on updates. CISA (KEV Catalog) requires applying patches for known exploited Exchange Server vulnerabilities within mandated timelines.

references.name: Exchange Server build numbers, release dates, and support status
CISA KEV Catalog: Apply patches for known exploited Exchange Server vulnerabilities within required timelines

remediation.description: Plan migration from Exchange 2016/2019 to Exchange Server SE and apply the latest supported updates.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: migrate Exchange 2016/2019 workloads to Exchange SE and apply current updates via approved maintenance process.

considerations: Migrating from Exchange 2016 or Exchange 2019 to Exchange SE requires environment planning and coexistence testing. Plan for sufficient coexistence time and test hybrid features, mail flow, and client connectivity before decommissioning older servers.

title: AD site count baseline
description: Total AD site count must be below Exchange performance warning thresholds: >= 750 sites is a warning, >= 1000 sites is a critical performance risk that degrades Exchange directory service lookups.

references.name: CSS ADSiteCount

remediation.description: Reduce the number of AD sites. As a workaround, increase the topology cache lifetime to 24 hours by changing ExchangeTopologyCacheLifetime to 1.00:00:00,00:20:00 in %ExchangeInstallPath%\Bin\Microsoft.Exchange.Directory.TopologyService.exe.config.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: investigate AD site count anomalies and Exchange topology impact.

considerations: Reducing the number of AD sites requires Active Directory topology changes that must be coordinated with the Active Directory team. Unnecessary sites may remain without functional impact but add administrative overhead.

title: Core Exchange services are running
description: All required Exchange Windows services (transport, information store, and helper services) must be in the Running state. Non-running required services cause mail flow interruptions and degraded functionality.

references.name: Microsoft Exchange services overview

remediation.description: Start critical Exchange services if they are stopped.
remediation.scriptTemplate: Start-Service -Name MSExchangeIS -ErrorAction Stop; Start-Service -Name MSExchangeTransport -ErrorAction Stop

considerations: Restarting Exchange services disrupts active client sessions and mail flow. Always use DAG maintenance mode or scheduled maintenance windows when restarting services on production servers.

title: Setting overrides baseline
description: No active Exchange setting overrides (Get-SettingOverride) should be present unless deliberately and temporarily applied. Active overrides can suppress critical security or functionality controls.

references.name: CSS SettingOverridesCheck
Deploy dedicated Exchange hybrid app
Exchange Server AMSI integration

remediation.description: Remove problematic Setting Overrides using Remove-SettingOverride. Review existing overrides with Get-SettingOverride and remove those flagged by the check. Only modify overrides as directed by Microsoft documentation or CSS support - incorrect usage can cause serious Exchange damage.
remediation.scriptTemplate: Get-SettingOverride | Format-List Name, ComponentName, SectionName, Status

considerations: Setting overrides are typically applied by Microsoft support to mitigate specific issues. Removing an override can re-expose the condition that originally required it. Review the purpose of each override with Microsoft before removing it. Exception: EnableSigningVerification is automatically set by Exchange when serialized data signing (EX-BP-054) is enabled and should not be treated as an issue. Exception: when the dedicated Exchange hybrid application is configured, the EnableExchangeHybrid3PAppFeature override and one FlightingServiceOverride_<ServerName>_F1.1[.x] override per Exchange server are expected and should not be treated as an issue. Exception: AMSI body scanning can be enabled per-protocol or for all protocols via setting overrides whose names start with EnableAMSIBodyScan (e.g., EnableAMSIBodyScanAllProtocols, EnableAMSIBodyScanForEcp, EnableAMSIBodyScanForEws, EnableAMSIBodyScanForOwa, EnableAMSIBodyScanForEcpEwsOwaPS); these overrides should not be treated as an issue when body scanning is in use. Exception: EnableEncryptionAlgorithmCBC enables AES256-CBC encryption mode for IRM-protected messages (EX-BP-153) and should not be treated as an issue.

title: Unified content cleanup baseline
description: The Exchange UnifiedContent temporary store cleanup must be correctly configured. Misconfigured cleanup causes progressive disk space accumulation in UnifiedContent folders under ExchangeTransport working directories.

references.name: CSS UnifiedContentCleanup

remediation.description: Add the correct UnifiedContent temp path to %ExchangeInstallPath%\Bin\Monitoring\Config\AntiMalware.xml to enable the monitoring probe to automatically clean up temp files from the EdgeTransport process.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: investigate and remediate Unified Content cleanup findings.

considerations: The Unified Content Cleanup task temporarily increases I/O load on the transport database volume when running. Schedule cleanup tasks during low-traffic periods. Cleaning a large accumulated backlog may take extended time.

title: Visual C++ redistributable version baseline
description: Visual C++ 2012 x64 and Visual C++ 2013 x64 redistributable runtimes must both be installed. Both are required by Exchange components; missing runtimes cause Exchange search and transport failures.

references.name: CSS VisualCRedistributableVersionCheck

remediation.description: Install the latest Visual C++ Redistributable version required for the installed Exchange server role from the Microsoft Visual C++ Redistributable Latest Supported Downloads page.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: install supported Visual C++ redistributable versions.

considerations: Updating Visual C++ redistributables typically does not require downtime but may require a server reboot. Only install versions validated by the Exchange Cumulative Update prerequisites documentation for the installed Exchange build.

title: CTS processor affinity baseline
description: CtsProcessorAffinityPercentage must be set to 0 (Exchange baseline). Any value above 0 restricts Exchange search/CTS to a subset of processors, degrading search performance and indexing throughput.

references.name: CSS CTSProcessorAffinityPercentageCheck

remediation.description: Remove or reset the CtsProcessorAffinityPercentage DWORD value to 0 under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\ExchangeServer\V15\Search\SystemParameters. This setting limits search performance and should not be used as a long-term fix.
remediation.scriptTemplate: Remove-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\ExchangeServer\V15\Search\SystemParameters' -Name CtsProcessorAffinityPercentage -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue

considerations: Changing the CTS processor affinity can reduce throughput on servers with many concurrent connections. This setting is rarely needed and should only be changed based on Microsoft support guidance. Incorrect configuration can degrade search performance.

title: Exchange database/log volume block size is 64KB
description: Volumes hosting Exchange mailbox database and log files should use 64KB allocation unit size (block size) for performance best practice.

references.name: Exchange storage best practices
Exchange CSS storage checks

remediation.description: Use 64KB allocation unit size on volumes that host Exchange database/log paths.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: reformat target volume with 64KB allocation unit and migrate Exchange database/log files.

considerations: Changing the block size of existing volumes requires reformatting — all data must be migrated to temporary storage, volumes reformatted, and data restored. This requires a planned maintenance window with database failovers. New volumes should be formatted with 64 KB blocks before use.

title: Exchange database/log volumes use NTFS or ReFS
description: Volumes hosting Exchange mailbox database and log paths should use supported filesystems NTFS or ReFS.

references.name: Exchange storage best practices
Exchange CSS storage guidance

remediation.description: Move Exchange database/log files to NTFS or ReFS-formatted volumes.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: provision NTFS/ReFS volumes and move databases/logs using Exchange maintenance procedures.

considerations: Converting an existing FAT32 volume to NTFS or ReFS requires data migration and reformatting if in-place conversion is not supported. ReFS offers resilience advantages but some Exchange administrators prefer NTFS for its wider tool compatibility. Verify ReFS is supported for the Exchange version before using it for database volumes.

title: High Performance power plan is active
description: Exchange servers should use the High Performance power plan to avoid CPU throttling and performance degradation.

references.name: CSS power plan recommendation
Exchange deployment and performance guidance

remediation.description: Set active power plan to High Performance.
remediation.scriptTemplate: powercfg /SETACTIVE 8c5e7fda-e8bf-4a96-9a85-a6e23a8c635c

considerations: Changing the power plan to High Performance increases power consumption on physical hosts. On virtual machines, the hypervisor controls CPU frequency and this setting has limited effect. Verify that your data center or cloud infrastructure supports and benefits from this configuration.

title: Hyper-Threading/SMT not enabled
description: Hyper-Threading (SMT) must be disabled: logical processor count must equal physical core count. Exchange does not support Hyper-Threading and its presence can significantly degrade performance.

references.name: CSS processor topology guidance

remediation.description: Review BIOS/host settings for SMT/Hyper-Threading and disable per performance policy where approved.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: BIOS/host-level change for SMT/Hyper-Threading.

considerations: Disabling Hyper-Threading/SMT is a mitigation for CPU side-channel vulnerabilities (MDS, TAA, Spectre). The performance reduction can be up to 30%. Only apply this if regulatory requirements or your threat model mandate it. This change requires a reboot and coordination with production scheduling.

title: NodeRunner memory limit baseline
description: NodeRunner process memory limit (memoryLimitMegabytes in noderunner.exe.config) must be set to 0. Non-zero values restrict Exchange search memory allocation and can cause search index failures.

references.name: CSS NodeRunnerMemoryLimitCheck

remediation.description: Set memoryLimitMegabytes to 0 in the noderunner.exe configuration file for optimal performance. Only apply a non-zero limit as a temporary workaround if the noderunner process is causing measurable server impact.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: review and adjust NodeRunner memory limit settings.

considerations: Adjusting the NodeRunner memory limit affects Exchange content indexing and search performance. Setting the limit too low can cause content index to lag or fail. The change takes effect after a service restart and should be tested against search responsiveness.

title: NUMA BIOS baseline
description: BIOS/boot NUMA group size optimization must be set to 0 (flat/clustered NUMA). Non-zero values (node interleaving) degrade Exchange memory access performance across NUMA nodes.

references.name: CSS NumaBiosCheck

remediation.description: Verify BIOS NUMA settings are configured so the OS can detect all processor cores. If only half the logical cores are visible to the OS, consult your server or OS vendor to correct the NUMA BIOS configuration.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: update NUMA BIOS options according to vendor documentation and Exchange baseline requirements.

considerations: BIOS-level NUMA configuration changes require server restart and must be coordinated with the hardware vendor. Incorrect NUMA configuration can degrade memory access performance on multi-socket systems.

title: OS volume filesystem is NTFS
description: The operating system volume should use NTFS for Exchange server operating system supportability and baseline consistency.

references.name: Exchange Server prerequisites and platform guidance

remediation.description: Use NTFS for the OS volume on Exchange servers.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: OS volume filesystem changes require server rebuild/reprovision planning.

considerations: The OS volume filesystem cannot be changed on a live server without reinstalling the operating system. This control is an environmental prerequisite when provisioning new Exchange servers.

title: Processor baseline
description: Logical processor count must not exceed the Exchange-supported maximum: 24 for Exchange 2016 and 48 for Exchange 2019/SE. Exceeding these thresholds can cause unexpected performance degradation.

references.name: CSS ProcessorCheck

remediation.description: If the CPU is throttled, verify the Power Plan is set to High Performance and change it immediately if not. Verify that the maximum processor speed reported matches the expected hardware specifications.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: review processor configuration against the documented Exchange baseline.

considerations: Processor replacement or configuration changes require hardware maintenance windows and potential load redistribution across the DAG. Verify the new processor meets Exchange performance guidance (not more than 4 cores per Hyper-Threaded processor in some Exchange sizing guides).

title: Receive Side Scaling (RSS) enabled
description: Receive Side Scaling (RSS) must be enabled on active network adapters. RSS distributes network receive processing across multiple processor cores, improving throughput for Exchange workloads.

references.name: CSS NIC/RSS checks

remediation.description: Enable RSS on adapters where supported.
remediation.scriptTemplate: Get-NetAdapterRss | Where-Object { -not $_.Enabled } | ForEach-Object { Enable-NetAdapterRss -Name $_.Name -Confirm:$false }

considerations: Enabling or reconfiguring RSS settings may require a network adapter driver update and a server reboot. Changes to RSS may briefly interrupt network connectivity. Verify that the network adapter firmware supports the target RSS configuration.

title: RPC minimum connection timeout baseline
description: Exchange RPC minimum connection timeout (RpcMinConnectionTimeout registry value) must be non-negative. A value of 0 is treated as not configured; a negative value is an invalid setting.

references.name: CSS RPCMinConnectionTimeoutCheck

remediation.description: Adjust the RPC MinConnectionTimeout registry setting to a more aggressive value than the OS defaults if network devices are dropping idle connections before the Exchange timeout triggers.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: review and adjust RPC minimum connection timeout settings.

considerations: Adjusting the RPC minimum connection timeout can affect Outlook MAPI connectivity behavior. Changes should be tested against Outlook profile connect/reconnect timing before applying to all servers.

title: Single page file configuration
description: Multiple configured page files can impact Exchange performance consistency. A single managed page file entry is recommended.

references.name: CSS page file warning logic

remediation.description: Review and standardize page file settings through approved OS baseline controls.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation recommended: align page file configuration with Exchange server baseline.

considerations: Exchange has specific page file sizing recommendations based on installed RAM. Changing page file settings requires a reboot. Placing the page file on the Exchange database volume is not recommended. Follow CSS and Microsoft guidance for page file placement and size.

title: Sleepy NIC baseline
description: NIC power management sleep/power-down settings must be disabled on all active adapters. Adapter power-down under low traffic causes intermittent connectivity interruptions for Exchange clients.

references.name: CSS SleepyNICCheck

remediation.description: Disable NIC power-saving by setting the PnPCapabilities DWORD value to 24 or 280 under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Class\{4D36E972-E325-11CE-BFC1-08002bE10318} for each affected network adapter.
remediation.scriptTemplate: Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Class\{4D36E972-E325-11CE-BFC1-08002bE10318}\NNNN' -Name PnPCapabilities -Value 24 -Type DWord

considerations: Disabling sleep and power-saving settings on network adapters prevents idle disconnects but may marginally increase power consumption. This setting is almost always the correct configuration for production Exchange servers. Requires testing after changing NIC advanced settings.

title: TCP/IP settings baseline
description: TCP KeepAliveTime registry value must be within the recommended exchange range of 900000 to 1800000 ms (15 to 30 minutes). Values outside this range (including the OS default 2-hour value) allow stale TCP connections to persist.

references.name: CSS TCPIPSettingsCheck

remediation.description: Set the KeepAliveTime DWORD value under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\TcpIp\Parameters to between 900000 and 1800000 milliseconds (15 to 30 minutes) to prevent idle TCP connections from being dropped prematurely.
remediation.scriptTemplate: Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip\Parameters' -Name KeepAliveTime -Value 900000 -Type DWord

considerations: TCP/IP stack changes (Chimney Offload, Large Send Offload, etc.) affect all network traffic on the server, not just Exchange. Some settings require a reboot. Verify that the current NIC driver supports the target configuration. Changes should be tested in a staging environment first.

title: VMXNET3 adapter health baseline (ESX)
description: For VMware ESX-hosted Exchange servers using VMXNET3, review discarded packet counters and verify ring size/buffer adapter properties are present and tuned per baseline. This control is warning-oriented (amber) in BestPractice reporting.

references.name: Exchange CSS performance checks
VMware VMXNET3 adapter guidance

remediation.description: Investigate VMXNET3 packet discards and validate ring/buffer advanced adapter settings against approved ESX/Exchange baseline.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: adjust VMXNET3 ring/buffer settings and resolve discard causes in host/guest network path.

considerations: Only applicable in VMware environments. Ensure VMware Tools is updated before changing the NIC type. If running on Hyper-V, Azure, or physical hardware, this check is not applicable. Changing the NIC type on a VM requires reconfiguring IP settings and may require OS-level NIC driver updates.

title: No expired Exchange certificates
description: DISA STIG EX19-MB-000019: Certificates used by Exchange services should not be expired.

references.name: Microsoft Exchange certificate procedures
DISA STIG EX19-MB-000019: Exchange servers must use approved DOD certificates (V-259649)

remediation.description: Replace expired certificates and assign them to Exchange services.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: issue/import certificate and run Enable-ExchangeCertificate.

considerations: Certificate renewal on an Exchange server requires updating the certificate on all Services (IIS, SMTP, IMAP, POP3) and removing the expired certificate. If using a third-party CA, plan lead time for certificate issuance (typically 1–5 business days). Internal clients may show SSL warnings until the new certificate is fully propagated via Directory Services.

title: Replication health checks pass
description: When DAG is deployed, replication health checks should pass.

references.name: Test-ReplicationHealth command

remediation.description: Investigate and resolve failed DAG replication checks.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required based on failing replication probes.

considerations: Replication failures may have causes beyond configuration — including storage latency, network connectivity, or witness server unavailability. Do not make DAG configuration changes during active failovers or when the DAG is already in a degraded state. Follow the resolution steps appropriate for each specific replication health check failure.

title: Accepted domains publish enforcing DMARC records
description: Each accepted domain should publish a valid DMARC record at _dmarc.<domain> with an enforcing policy (quarantine or reject). CISA (BOD 18-01 §d(3)) requires configuring DMARC with a policy of reject. CIS Controls v8 (Control 9.5 IG1) requires implementing DMARC and enabling receiver-side verification.

references.name: RFC 7489 - Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting, and Conformance (DMARC)
NIS2 Directive (EU) - cybersecurity risk-management measures
CISA BOD 18-01 (2017): Enhance Email and Web Security — §d(3): Configure DMARC with a policy of reject
CIS Controls v8, Control 9.5 (IG1): Implement DMARC and Enable Receiver-Side Verification

remediation.description: Publish one DMARC TXT record and move policy to quarantine or reject after monitoring.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # DNS change required at _dmarc.<domain>, e.g. v=DMARC1; p=quarantine; rua=mailto:dmarc-reports@example.com

considerations: Enforcing DMARC p=reject blocks mail that fails SPF and/or DKIM alignment, including forwarded mail and mailing lists that rewrite DMARC-aligned identities. Begin with p=none (monitoring mode) and review DMARC aggregate reports before moving to p=quarantine, then p=reject. Ensure DKIM is fully deployed before switching to reject policy.

title: Accepted domains publish SMTP DANE TLSA for MX hosts
description: For each MX host serving accepted domains, SMTP DANE should be configured by publishing TLSA records at _25._tcp.<mx-host>. CISA (BOD 18-01) requires enforcing SMTP encryption for email transport.

references.name: RFC 7672 - SMTP Security via Opportunistic DANE TLS
NIS2 Directive (EU) - cybersecurity risk-management measures
CISA BOD 18-01 (2017): Enhance Email and Web Security — enforce SMTP encryption for email transport

remediation.description: Publish TLSA records for all MX targets and maintain DNSSEC-signed DNS zones for DANE trust validation.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # DNS change required: publish TLSA records at _25._tcp.<mx-host> and ensure DNSSEC chain of trust.

considerations: DANE requires DNSSEC to be enabled on the MX domain and TLSA records published in DNS. Many DNS providers do not support DNSSEC/DANE. Verify that your external DNS provider supports these features before attempting implementation. Incorrect TLSA records will cause legitimate inbound mail to be rejected by DANE-validating sending servers.

title: Accepted domains publish valid MTA-STS policies
description: Each accepted domain should publish a valid MTA-STS DNS TXT record and a reachable HTTPS policy file with valid STSv1 syntax. CISA (BOD 18-01) requires enforcing email transport security via policy.

references.name: RFC 8461 - SMTP MTA Strict Transport Security (MTA-STS)
NIS2 Directive (EU) - cybersecurity risk-management measures
CISA BOD 18-01 (2017): Enhance Email and Web Security — enforce email transport security via policy

remediation.description: Publish _mta-sts TXT with v=STSv1; id=... and host a valid policy at https://mta-sts.<domain>/.well-known/mta-sts.txt.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # DNS + web hosting change required for MTA-STS policy publication.

considerations: MTA-STS in enforce mode causes sending servers that cannot verify TLS to bounce messages rather than deliver them in plaintext. Always begin with mode=testing and monitor policy evaluation before switching to enforce. The max_age TTL determines how long senders cache the policy — a shorter TTL allows faster rollback but reduces caching efficiency.

title: Accepted domains publish valid SPF records
description: Each accepted domain should publish exactly one syntactically valid SPF TXT record (v=spf1) with a terminal all mechanism and within SPF lookup limits. CISA (BOD 18-01 §d(1)) requires configuring SPF for all domains. CIS Controls v8 (Control 9.5 IG1) requires implementing DMARC and enabling receiver-side verification.

references.name: RFC 7208 - Sender Policy Framework (SPF)
NIS2 Directive (EU) - cybersecurity risk-management measures
CISA BOD 18-01 (2017): Enhance Email and Web Security — §d(1): Configure SPF for all domains
CIS Controls v8, Control 9.5 (IG1): Implement DMARC and Enable Receiver-Side Verification

remediation.description: Publish one SPF TXT record per accepted domain with only authorized senders and an explicit -all or ~all policy.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # DNS change required: create one TXT at the zone apex, e.g. v=spf1 include:mail.example.com -all

considerations: A hard-fail SPF record (v=spf1 ... -all) causes receiving servers to reject mail that does not pass SPF. Soft-fail (~all) marks mail without rejecting it. Before changing from softfail to hardfail, ensure all legitimate sending sources (bulk mail services, line-of-business applications, forwarding servers) are included in the SPF record. Incomplete SPF records will cause legitimate mail to be blocked.

title: AMSI integration baseline
description: At least one AMSI (Antimalware Scan Interface) provider must be registered and AMSI must not be disabled by Exchange setting override. AMSI enables real-time scanning of Exchange HTTP requests against antimalware engines. CISA (AA21-062A) recommends enabling AMSI integration to detect and block malicious scripts.

references.name: CSS AMSIIntegration
CISA AA21-062A: Mitigate Microsoft Exchange Server Vulnerabilities — enable AMSI integration to detect and block malicious scripts

remediation.description: Ensure AMSI integration is enabled and no Setting Overrides exist that disable it. If Body Scanning is enabled, verify it is supported on the installed Exchange build version.
remediation.scriptTemplate: Get-SettingOverride | Where-Object { $_.SectionName -like '*AMSI*' } | Format-List

considerations: Some third-party antivirus products that replace the Windows Defender AMSI provider may not register an AMSI provider even when active. Verify AMSI integration compatibility with your antivirus vendor before enforcing this control. Incorrectly disabling AMSI can leave Exchange endpoints exposed to script-based attacks.

title: Auth certificate baseline
description: The Exchange OAuth authentication certificate must be present in LocalMachine\My and must have at least 30 days remaining before expiry. An expired or missing auth certificate breaks OAuth and hybrid authentication flows.

references.name: CSS AuthCertificateCheck

remediation.description: Verify the Auth Certificate is valid and present on all Exchange servers. If the primary Auth Certificate was replaced without rerunning the Hybrid Configuration Wizard, re-run the wizard to sync the Auth Certificate with Azure AD.
remediation.scriptTemplate: Get-AuthConfig | Format-List CurrentCertificateThumbprint, PreviousCertificateThumbprint

considerations: The OAuth authentication certificate is required for hybrid features (free/busy lookup, mail routing to Exchange Online Archive, cross-premises calendar delegation). Replacing the Auth certificate requires coordinated updates on all Exchange servers and a brief interruption to hybrid connectivity. Follow the Microsoft Auth Certificate renewal procedure precisely.

title: Credential Guard disabled on Exchange servers
description: Credential Guard is currently not supported on Exchange servers and can introduce performance impact. Applicability: Windows Server 2019/2022/2025 benchmark scope (build 17763+), including Server 2025 where Credential Guard may be enabled by default.

references.name: CSS Credential Guard check
Manage Windows Defender Credential Guard

remediation.description: Disable Credential Guard, which is not supported on Exchange Server. Remove it via Windows Defender Credential Guard Group Policy or by modifying the Lsa registry key to eliminate the associated performance impact on Exchange.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation recommended: disable Credential Guard (for example set LsaCfgFlags to 0 and DeviceGuard CredentialGuard policy to disabled) and reboot the server.

considerations: Credential Guard is intentionally not recommended on Exchange servers because it can interfere with Exchange service authentication. This control documents the expected state (disabled). Do not enable Credential Guard on Exchange hosts without explicit Microsoft support guidance.

title: EEMS baseline
description: The Exchange Emergency Mitigation Service (MSExchangeMitigation) must be Running with Automatic start mode and mitigations enabled. EEMS enables Microsoft to deliver emergency mitigations for critical Exchange vulnerabilities. CISA (AA21-062A) recommends enabling the Microsoft Exchange Emergency Mitigation Service.

references.name: CSS EEMSCheck
CISA AA21-062A: Mitigate Microsoft Exchange Server Vulnerabilities — enable the Microsoft Exchange Emergency Mitigation Service

remediation.description: Ensure the MSExchangeMitigation Windows service is set to Automatic startup and is in a Running state. Verify the server has outbound connectivity to the Office Configuration Service (OCS) mitigation endpoint at officeclient.microsoft.com.
remediation.scriptTemplate: Get-Service MSExchangeMitigation | Format-List Name, Status, StartType

considerations: EEMS automatically applies mitigations from Microsoft without requiring manual patching. If organizational policy requires change control approval before applying any system changes, EEMS may not be appropriate in enforce mode. Review EEMS logs after automatic mitigations are applied to assess impact.

title: Exchange computer membership baseline
description: The Exchange server computer account must be a member of the Exchange Servers and Exchange Trusted Subsystem Active Directory security groups. Missing group membership causes permission failures across Exchange management operations.

references.name: CSS ExchangeComputerMembership

remediation.description: Add the Exchange server's computer object to both the Exchange Trusted Subsystem and Exchange Servers universal security groups in Active Directory. Perform an IISReset after updating group membership.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: align Exchange computer account memberships with support guidance.

considerations: Exchange Server computer accounts must be members of the Exchange Servers universal security group for correct service operation. Removing Exchange from this group will break Exchange service functionality. This control is informational — remediation should only be performed if the membership was accidentally altered.

title: Exchange Hybrid Application baseline
description: Validate Exchange Hybrid Application configuration.

references.name: CSS ExchangeHybridApplicationCheck

remediation.description: Deploy a dedicated Exchange Hybrid Application using the ConfigureExchangeHybridApplication tool to support the transition from EWS to Microsoft Graph API for hybrid functionality.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: reconfigure Exchange Hybrid Application settings based on detected findings.

considerations: The Exchange Hybrid Application is used by Exchange Online to communicate with on-premises Exchange. Removing or changing the hybrid application configuration will disrupt hybrid mail flow, free/busy lookups, and cross-premises mailbox moves. Any reconfiguration must be coordinated with Exchange Online and verified in the Exchange Admin Center.

title: Extended Protection enabled on Exchange virtual directories
description: Extended Protection should be enabled on critical Exchange IIS virtual directories where supported. CISA (AA21-062A) requires enabling Extended Protection for Authentication on all Exchange virtual directories.

references.name: Microsoft Exchange Extended Protection guidance
NIS2 Directive (EU)
CIS Microsoft Exchange Server Benchmark
CIS Microsoft Exchange Server 2019 Benchmark v1.0.0: Ensure Extended Protection is Enabled on Exchange virtual directories
CISA AA21-062A: Mitigate Microsoft Exchange Server Vulnerabilities — enable Extended Protection for Authentication on all Exchange virtual directories

remediation.description: Enable Extended Protection per Microsoft guidance for each virtual directory.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation recommended due to environment-specific prerequisites.

considerations: Enabling Extended Protection can break Outlook connectivity in some older hybrid configurations and may require configuration changes to specific virtual directories (e.g., EWS, AutoDiscover). Microsoft recommends thorough testing in a non-production environment first. Some older Outlook clients and third-party EWS integrations may not be compatible with Extended Protection.

title: FIP-FS baseline
description: DISA STIG EX19-MB-000146 / EX19-MB-000147 (Exchange 2019): FIP-FS engine version folders must not contain known-problematic version markers. Certain FIP-FS engine versions cause Exchange transport queue failures and mail flow stoppage.

references.name: CSS FIPFSCheck
DISA STIG EX19-MB-000146: Exchange antimalware agent must be enabled and configured (V-259694)
DISA STIG EX19-MB-000147: The Exchange malware scanning agent must be configured for automatic updates (V-259695)

remediation.description: Run Reset-ScanEngineVersion.ps1 from the CSS-Exchange toolkit to remove the problematic FIP-FS scan engine version folder (2201010000 or higher). On fixed Exchange builds, delete the folder manually or restart the Transport service.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: resolve detected FIP-FS warnings/errors.

considerations: FIP-FS (File Inspection for Filtering) is used by the Exchange transport pipeline for content filtering. FIP-FS failures typically indicate scan engine issues. Repair or reinstallation of the scan engine may temporarily disable content filtering. Review impact on transport rules and malware filtering before proceeding.

title: IIS web.config baseline
description: All Exchange IIS web.config files must be present and contain valid XML. Missing or corrupt web.config files cause failures in virtual directories including OWA, ECP, EWS, and Autodiscover.

references.name: CSS IISWebConfigCheck

remediation.description: Replace all %ExchangeInstallDir% placeholder tokens in Exchange web.config and SharedWebConfig.config files with the actual Exchange installation path from the registry at HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\ExchangeServer\v15\Setup\MsiInstallPath.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: align IIS web.config entries with Exchange baseline.

considerations: Modifying IIS web.config files for Exchange virtual directories can break authentication, HTTPS bindings, or client connectivity. Only apply changes that are explicitly documented in Exchange configuration guidance. Back up web.config files before making any manual changes.

title: Internal transport certificate baseline
description: The Exchange internal transport certificate must be present in LocalMachine\My and must have at least 30 days remaining before expiry. An expired internal transport certificate breaks server-to-server mail delivery.

references.name: CSS InternalTransportCertificateCheck

remediation.description: If the Internal Transport Certificate is missing or expired, create a new one using New-ExchangeCertificate with the server NetBIOS name, FQDN, and SMTP service assignment. Run Enable-ExchangeCertificate -Services SMTP after confirming propagation to all Exchange servers.
remediation.scriptTemplate: New-ExchangeCertificate -PrivateKeyExportable $false -Services SMTP -SubjectName 'CN=ServerFQDN' -DomainName 'ServerFQDN','ServerNetBIOSName'

considerations: The internal transport certificate is used for Exchange-to-Exchange SMTP TLS. Replacing it requires the certificate to be propagated to all Exchange servers in the organization before activation. A brief interruption to inter-server TLS may occur during certificate rollover.

title: IPv6 enabled baseline
description: IPv6 must not be fully disabled (DisabledComponents registry value must not be 255 = 0xFF). Exchange 2013 and later require IPv6 for internal communication; fully disabling IPv6 causes transport and directory failures.

references.name: CSS IPv6EnabledCheck

remediation.description: If disabling IPv6, disable it consistently both on the NIC adapters and in the registry under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip6\Parameters (DisabledComponents). Partial disabling causes more problems than leaving IPv6 enabled.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: align IPv6 configuration with the supported Exchange baseline.

considerations: Completely disabling IPv6 at the OS level is not recommended by Microsoft and can cause issues with Windows Server networking stack and Active Directory on Server 2019 and later. Use the Exchange CSS guidance — which configures specific IPv6 bindings — rather than disabling IPv6 entirely. Exchange Server itself does not require IPv6 but the Windows stack does.

title: LLMNR disabled by policy
description: LLMNR should be disabled to reduce spoofing and downgrade risks. Applicability: domain-joined Exchange servers. Exception: isolated legacy environments with compensating DNS protections. CIS (Section 18.5.4.2 L1) requires 'Turn off multicast name resolution' (LLMNR) to be set to 'Enabled'.

references.name: CIS Microsoft Windows Server Benchmark
CIS Microsoft Windows Server 2022 Benchmark v3.0.0, Section 18.5.4.2 (L1): Ensure 'Turn off multicast name resolution' (LLMNR) is set to 'Enabled'

remediation.description: Disable LLMNR using DNS client policy registry key.
remediation.scriptTemplate: New-Item -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\DNSClient' -Force | Out-Null; Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\DNSClient' -Name EnableMulticast -Type DWord -Value 0

considerations: Disabling LLMNR requires a Group Policy change. This affects all Windows name resolution on the network segment, not just Exchange. LLMNR is commonly exploited in NTLM relay attack scenarios (e.g., Responder). Disabling LLMNR is low-risk for environments with properly configured DNS.

title: NTLMv2 authentication enforcement baseline
description: LAN Manager authentication level and NTLM minimum session security must be configured together to enforce NTLMv2 only and refuse LM/NTLM. Applicability: Exchange 2016/2019/SE with modern clients. Exception: validated legacy interoperability requirements must be risk accepted. CIS (Section 2.3.11.7 L1) requires 'Network security: LAN Manager authentication level' to be set to 'Send NTLMv2 response only. Refuse LM & NTLM' (LmCompatibilityLevel = 5). CIS (Section 2.3.11.10 L1) requires minimum session security for NTLM SSP clients, and CIS (Section 2.3.11.11 L1) for servers, both set to 'Require NTLMv2 session security, Require 128-bit encryption' (NtlmMinClientSec and NtlmMinServerSec = 537395200).

references.name: CIS Microsoft Windows Server Benchmark
Network security: LAN Manager authentication level
CIS Microsoft Windows Server 2022 Benchmark v3.0.0, Section 2.3.11.7 (L1): Ensure 'Network security: LAN Manager authentication level' is set to 'Send NTLMv2 response only. Refuse LM & NTLM'
Network security: minimum session security for NTLM SSP based clients and servers

CIS Microsoft Windows Server 2022 Benchmark v3.0.0, Section 2.3.11.10 (L1): Ensure 'Network security: Minimum session security for NTLM SSP based (including secure RPC) clients' is set to 'Require NTLMv2 session security, Require 128-bit encryption'

CIS Microsoft Windows Server 2022 Benchmark v3.0.0, Section 2.3.11.11 (L1): Ensure 'Network security: Minimum session security for NTLM SSP based (including secure RPC) servers' is set to 'Require NTLMv2 session security, Require 128-bit encryption'

remediation.description: Set LmCompatibilityLevel to 5 (refuse LM and NTLM, send NTLMv2), and set NtlmMinClientSec and NtlmMinServerSec to require NTLMv2 session security with 128-bit encryption.
remediation.scriptTemplate:
# Enforce NTLMv2 only - refuse LM and NTLM (CIS 2.3.11.7)
Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa' -Name LmCompatibilityLevel -Type DWord -Value 5

# Require NTLMv2 session security + 128-bit encryption for NTLM SSP clients (CIS 2.3.11.10)
Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\MSV1_0' -Name NtlmMinClientSec -Type DWord -Value 537395200

# Require NTLMv2 session security + 128-bit encryption for NTLM SSP servers (CIS 2.3.11.11)
Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\MSV1_0' -Name NtlmMinServerSec -Type DWord -Value 537395200

considerations: Setting LAN Manager authentication level to NTLMv2-only will break authentication for legacy clients and systems that only support LM or NTLM authentication. Requiring NTLM 128-bit session encryption drops connections from legacy clients that only support 56-bit encryption (pre-Windows 2000). Verify all clients, servers, and services that authenticate against Exchange or AD support NTLMv2 and 128-bit session security before enforcing these policies. Test against any legacy NTLM-dependent services before applying.

title: Microsoft Defender antivirus exclusions configured for Exchange
description: DISA STIG EX19-MB-000134 (HIGH): When Microsoft Defender real-time protection is active on an Exchange server, the recommended folder and process exclusions must be configured to prevent Defender from interfering with Exchange databases, transport queues, and critical processes. Required exclusions are version-dependent: Exchange 2016 adds Unified Messaging paths; Exchange 2019 and SE do not include those paths. CISA (AA21-062A) recommends configuring Microsoft Defender antivirus with the correct Exchange-specific exclusions.

references.name: Running Windows antivirus software on Exchange servers
CISA AA21-062A: Mitigate Microsoft Exchange Server Vulnerabilities — configure Microsoft Defender antivirus with correct Exchange-specific exclusions
DISA STIG EX19-MB-000134 (HIGH): Exchange servers must have an approved DOD email-aware virus protection software installed (V-259686)
CSS-Exchange Set-ExchAVExclusions script

remediation.description:
Use the CSS-Exchange Set-ExchAVExclusions.ps1 script to automatically configure all required Microsoft Defender exclusions for the installed Exchange version. Run from an elevated Exchange Management Shell on each Exchange server:

  .\Set-ExchAVExclusions.ps1

The script auto-detects the Exchange install path and version and sets all recommended folder and process exclusions. To also validate existing exclusions without making changes, add the -CheckExclusions switch:

  .\Set-ExchAVExclusions.ps1 -CheckExclusions

The script is available from https://aka.ms/ExchAVExclusions. Alternatively, add exclusions manually using Set-MpPreference or Add-MpPreference — verify paths match the Exchange install path for the installed version.
remediation.scriptTemplate:
# Download and run Set-ExchAVExclusions.ps1 (requires elevated Exchange Management Shell)
# https://aka.ms/ExchAVExclusions

# Validate current exclusions only:
# .\Set-ExchAVExclusions.ps1 -CheckExclusions

# Apply all recommended exclusions:
# .\Set-ExchAVExclusions.ps1

# Review currently configured exclusions:
Get-MpPreference | Select-Object -ExpandProperty ExclusionPath
Get-MpPreference | Select-Object -ExpandProperty ExclusionProcess

considerations: Microsoft Defender antivirus exclusions for Exchange must be kept current as Exchange paths evolve across Cumulative Updates. Incorrect or overly broad exclusions can leave transport databases and IIS directories unprotected. Exclusions should be validated against the current Microsoft-recommended list for the installed Exchange version.

title: Modern Authentication enabled
description: Modern Authentication should be enabled for Exchange to reduce legacy authentication exposure. CISA (AA21-062A) recommends enabling Modern Authentication (OAuth 2.0) and disabling Basic Authentication.

references.name: Enable Modern Authentication in Exchange on-premises
CISA AA21-062A: Mitigate Microsoft Exchange Server Vulnerabilities — enable Modern Authentication (OAuth 2.0) and disable Basic Authentication

remediation.description: Enable OAuth2 client profile for Exchange organization.
remediation.scriptTemplate: Set-OrganizationConfig -OAuth2ClientProfileEnabled $true

considerations: Enabling Modern Authentication requires all connected clients to support OAuth 2.0. Some older versions of Outlook (pre-2013) and third-party EWS or MAPI applications may not support Modern Authentication and will stop connecting after Basic Authentication is disabled. Audit all connected client types before enabling Modern Authentication and disabling Basic Auth.

title: OWA Download Domains configured
description: OWA Download Domains (ExternalDownloadHostName) must be configured. Without a separate download domain, OWA attachment rendering shares origin with ECP/OWA, creating cross-site scripting (XSS) attack surface. CISA (AA21-062A) recommends configuring OWA Download Domains to prevent NTLM relay attacks.

references.name: OWA Download Domains configuration guidance
CISA AA21-062A: Mitigate Microsoft Exchange Server Vulnerabilities — configure OWA Download Domains to prevent NTLM relay attacks

remediation.description: Configure OWA download domains according to Exchange security best practices.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: set OWA DownloadDomains values and validate client access behavior.

considerations: Configuring OWA Download Domains creates a separate subdomain (e.g., attachments.domain.com) for serving attachment content. This requires DNS configuration and possibly additional SSL certificate coverage for the new subdomain. After configuration, browser clients will be redirected to the attachment domain when downloading from OWA.

title: POP3 and IMAP services disabled unless explicitly required
description: DISA STIG EX19-MB-000065 / EX19-MB-000066: Legacy mailbox protocols should be disabled by default to limit unnecessary attack surface. CIS (Section 2.4.1 L1) requires 'POP3' Windows services to be set to 'Disabled', and CIS (Section 2.4.2 L1) requires 'IMAP4' Windows services to be set to 'Disabled'.

references.name: Exchange POP3 and IMAP4 service management
CIS Microsoft Exchange Server Benchmark
CIS Microsoft Exchange Server 2019 Benchmark v1.0.0, Section 2.4.1 (L1): Ensure 'POP3' Windows services are set to 'Disabled'
CIS Microsoft Exchange Server 2019 Benchmark v1.0.0, Section 2.4.2 (L1): Ensure 'IMAP4' Windows services are set to 'Disabled'
DISA STIG EX19-MB-000065: The Exchange IMAP4 service must be disabled (V-259667)
DISA STIG EX19-MB-000066: The Exchange POP3 service must be disabled (V-259668)

remediation.description: Disable POP3 and IMAP4 Exchange services when not required.
remediation.scriptTemplate: Set-Service -Name MSExchangePOP3 -StartupType Disabled; Stop-Service -Name MSExchangePOP3 -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue; Set-Service -Name MSExchangePOP3BE -StartupType Disabled; Stop-Service -Name MSExchangePOP3BE -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue; Set-Service -Name MSExchangeIMAP4 -StartupType Disabled; Stop-Service -Name MSExchangeIMAP4 -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue; Set-Service -Name MSExchangeIMAP4BE -StartupType Disabled; Stop-Service -Name MSExchangeIMAP4BE -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue

considerations: If POP3 or IMAP4 services are actively used by clients (mobile devices, legacy mail clients, monitoring systems), disabling them will cause connectivity failures. Audit client usage before disabling these services. If these services are required, ensure they are explicitly permitted and secured with TLS.

title: PowerShell execution policy is not unrestricted
description: DISA STIG EX19-MB-000061: PowerShell execution policy must not be set to Unrestricted or Bypass on Exchange servers. Permissive execution policies allow unsigned scripts to run freely, increasing risk of malicious script execution.

references.name: about_Execution_Policies
DISA STIG EX19-MB-000061: Exchange local machine policy must require signed scripts (V-259664)

remediation.description: Set local machine execution policy to RemoteSigned.
remediation.scriptTemplate: Set-ExecutionPolicy -Scope LocalMachine -ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned -Force

considerations: Changing the PowerShell execution policy to AllSigned or RemoteSigned will prevent unsigned scripts from running. Audit all scripts used for Exchange management and automation before enforcing a restrictive policy. Scripts used by monitoring tools or automation systems may fail if not properly signed.

title: Serialized data signing baseline
description: Exchange serialized data signing (EnableSerializationDataSigning registry value) must be enabled. Serialized data signing prevents deserialization-based attacks against Exchange endpoint processing. CISA (AA21-062A) recommends enabling serialized data signing to prevent deserialization attacks.

references.name: CSS SerializedDataSigningCheck
CISA AA21-062A: Mitigate Microsoft Exchange Server Vulnerabilities — enable serialized data signing to prevent deserialization attacks

remediation.description: Ensure PowerShell Serialization Payload Signing is enabled on Exchange 2016 CU23+ or Exchange 2019 CU12+ with January 2023 SU or later installed. Verify the signing certificate is valid and present on all Exchange servers.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: configure serialized data signing to the documented baseline.

considerations: Serialized data signing requires a minimum Exchange Cumulative Update level (Exchange 2019 CU12+ or Exchange SE). Enabling it on an older build that does not fully support the feature may cause issues. Verify supported build version before enabling. After enabling, a brief transport interruption may occur while services restart.

title: SMBv1 server protocol is disabled
description: SMBv1 should be disabled on Exchange hosts. Applicability: Exchange 2016/2019/SE on Windows Server 2016/2019. Exception: legacy NAS or application dependencies requiring SMB1 must be explicitly documented and isolated. CIS Controls v8 (Control 4.8 IG1) requires uninstalling or disabling unnecessary services on enterprise assets.

references.name: CIS Microsoft Windows Server Benchmark
SMB security hardening guidance
CIS Controls v8, Control 4.8 (IG1): Uninstall or Disable Unnecessary Services on Enterprise Assets and Software

remediation.description: Disable SMBv1 using the SMB server configuration cmdlet and remove the Windows optional feature.
remediation.scriptTemplate:
Set-SmbServerConfiguration -EnableSMB1Protocol $false -Force
Disable-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online -FeatureName SMB1Protocol -NoRestart

considerations: Disabling SMBv1 will prevent file sharing with Windows XP and Windows Server 2003 clients or servers. In modern environments this is typically safe. Verify there are no legacy systems (e.g., old printers, NAS devices, or monitoring appliances) on the network that require SMBv1 before disabling.

title: TLS 1.0 and TLS 1.1 are disabled
description: DISA STIG EX19-MB-000007: Legacy TLS protocol versions should be disabled on Exchange servers.

references.name: Microsoft TLS best practices
NIS2 Directive (EU) 2022/2555
CIS Microsoft Windows Server Benchmark
CIS Microsoft Windows Server 2022 Benchmark v3.0.0 (L1): Ensure TLS 1.0 is disabled (Enabled = 0) under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\SCHANNEL\Protocols\TLS 1.0
CIS Microsoft Windows Server 2022 Benchmark v3.0.0 (L1): Ensure TLS 1.1 is disabled (Enabled = 0) under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\SCHANNEL\Protocols\TLS 1.1
DISA STIG EX19-MB-000007: Exchange must use encryption for Outlook Web App (OWA) access (V-259646)

remediation.description: Disable TLS 1.0 and TLS 1.1 for both Server and Client sides in SCHANNEL, creating keys if absent and setting DisabledByDefault.

considerations: Disabling TLS 1.0 and TLS 1.1 can break connectivity with legacy clients, monitoring systems, and SMTP relay partners that do not support TLS 1.2. Audit all TLS-dependent applications, relay connectors, and partner connectors before disabling. Monitor Exchange transport logs after enforcement for TLS handshake failures.

title: TLS 1.2 is enabled
description: DISA STIG EX19-MB-000236 (Exchange 2019): TLS 1.2 should be enabled for Exchange and related server communications.

references.name: Microsoft TLS 1.2 deployment guidance
CIS Microsoft Windows Server Benchmark

CIS Microsoft Windows Server 2022 Benchmark v3.0.0 (L1): Ensure TLS 1.2 is enabled (Enabled = 1, DisabledByDefault = 0) under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\SCHANNEL\Protocols\TLS 1.2
DISA STIG EX19-MB-000236 (HIGH): The application must protect the confidentiality and integrity of transmitted information (V-259710)

remediation.description: Enable TLS 1.2 for both Server and Client sides in SCHANNEL, creating keys if absent and clearing DisabledByDefault.

considerations: Enabling TLS 1.2 is generally low-risk on modern systems. However, if TLS 1.0 and 1.1 are simultaneously disabled, applications or services that do not support TLS 1.2 will fail. Test dependent systems before disabling older protocol versions alongside enabling TLS 1.2.

title: TLS 1.3 is disabled for Exchange compatibility
description: For current on-premises Exchange baselines, TLS 1.3 should remain disabled on supported Windows Server versions due to known Exchange compatibility risks highlighted by Microsoft CSS. Applicability: supported OS builds only.

references.name: TLS registry settings (Windows Server)
NIS2 Directive (EU) - cybersecurity risk-management measures
CIS Microsoft Windows Server Benchmark
EDPB Guidelines, Recommendations, Best Practices

remediation.description: Disable TLS 1.3 server protocol in SCHANNEL on supported Windows Server versions for Exchange compatibility.
remediation.scriptTemplate: New-Item -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\SCHANNEL\Protocols\TLS 1.3\Server' -Force | Out-Null; Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\SCHANNEL\Protocols\TLS 1.3\Server' -Name Enabled -Type DWord -Value 0; Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\SCHANNEL\Protocols\TLS 1.3\Server' -Name DisabledByDefault -Type DWord -Value 1

considerations: TLS 1.3 is currently not supported by Exchange Server transport. Enabling TLS 1.3 at the OS Schannel level will not cause Exchange SMTP issues but Exchange will not use it for mail transport. IIS may use TLS 1.3 for HTTPS connections if IIS supports it, which is generally safe on Server 2022.

title: Trusted root certificates baseline
description: The DisableRootAutoUpdate registry policy must not be set to 1. When set to 1, the Windows trusted root certificate store cannot update automatically, causing certificate validation failures for TLS and OAuth.

references.name: CSS TrustedRootCertificatesCheck

remediation.description: Enable automatic root certificate updates via Group Policy or manually install required root certificates. Ensure the DigiCert Global Root G2 certificate is installed on all Exchange servers before March 15, 2026 to prevent certificate validation failures.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: align trusted root certificate store with enterprise certificate management baseline.

considerations: Automatic root certificate updates require outbound HTTPS access to Windows Update. In isolated environments, certificates must be manually distributed. The DigiCert Global Root G2 certificate is required by Microsoft 365 and Azure services; its absence after March 15, 2026 will cause TLS trust chain failures.

title: Vulnerability baseline
description: DISA STIG EX19-MB-000283: Exchange version must be at Exchange SE lifecycle baseline. Exchange 2016 and Exchange 2019 reached end of mainstream support and represent lifecycle-based vulnerability exposure that requires migration to Exchange SE.

references.name: CSS VulnerabilityCheck
DISA STIG EX19-MB-000283: Exchange must be configured in accordance with DOD security configuration settings based on STIGs, NSA guides, CTOs, and DTMs (V-259712)

remediation.description: Apply the latest Exchange security updates to remediate known vulnerabilities. Address specific CVEs flagged by the check including SMBv3 (CVE-2020-0796), .NET (CVE-2020-1147), and download domain issues (CVE-2021-1730).
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: address all reported vulnerability findings.

considerations: Applying Exchange security patches requires scheduled downtime and thorough regression testing. Patches may change default configuration, IIS bindings, or service behavior. Use DAG maintenance mode and test mail flow and client connectivity after each patch.

title: Windows Firewall enabled for all profiles
description: Domain, Private, and Public firewall profiles should remain enabled. Applicability: all Exchange server roles. Exception: temporary troubleshooting windows approved through change control. CIS (Sections 9.1.1, 9.2.1, and 9.3.1 L1) requires Windows Firewall to be enabled for all three profiles: Domain, Private, and Public.

references.name: CIS Microsoft Windows Server Benchmark
Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security
CIS Microsoft Windows Server 2022 Benchmark v3.0.0, Section 9.1.1 (L1): Ensure 'Windows Firewall: Domain: Firewall state' is set to 'On'
CIS Microsoft Windows Server 2022 Benchmark v3.0.0, Section 9.2.1 (L1): Ensure 'Windows Firewall: Private: Firewall state' is set to 'On'
CIS Microsoft Windows Server 2022 Benchmark v3.0.0, Section 9.3.1 (L1): Ensure 'Windows Firewall: Public: Firewall state' is set to 'On'

remediation.description: Enable all Windows Firewall profiles.
remediation.scriptTemplate: Set-NetFirewallProfile -Profile Domain,Private,Public -Enabled True

considerations: The Windows Firewall must include all Exchange-required inbound and outbound port rules before enabling. Missing rules will block SMTP transport, client access, or management traffic. Review Exchange port requirements for all installed roles before enabling the firewall. Monitor event logs for blocked connections after enforcement.

title: Receive connector internal and external relay patterns are separated
description: DISA STIG EX19-MB-000135: Receive connectors should follow clear internal versus external relay/authentication patterns and avoid broad anonymous relay exposure.

references.name: Receive connectors in Exchange Server
DISA STIG EX19-MB-000135: Exchange internal receive connectors must not allow anonymous connections (V-259687)

remediation.description: Review and redesign receive connectors to separate internal and external relay/auth patterns.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: adjust connector scopes, permission groups, and TLS/auth settings.

considerations: Restricting or reconfiguring receive connectors can block legitimate SMTP relay from applications and devices. Carefully audit all SMTP relay sources before changing connector permissions or authentication settings. Test mail flow from all relay sources after applying changes.

title: Transport retry configuration baseline
description: Transport retry settings must meet recommended thresholds: MaxPerDomainOutboundConnections >= 40 and MessageRetryInterval <= 5 minutes. Below-minimum connection limits and long retry intervals cause mail delivery delays to high-volume domains.

references.name: CSS TransportRetryConfigCheck

remediation.description: Use Set-TransportService to set MaxPerDomainOutboundConnections to 40 or higher and MessageRetryInterval to 5 minutes or less to prevent mail queue buildup during delivery retries.
remediation.scriptTemplate: Set-TransportService -Identity (hostname) -MaxPerDomainOutboundConnections 40 -MessageRetryInterval 00:05:00

considerations: Adjusting transport retry configuration can affect how quickly Exchange requeues messages after a transient failure. Changes to retry intervals impact end-user mail delivery experience during disruptions. Follow Microsoft guidance for retry values — overly aggressive retries increase load on target servers.

title: Microsoft Defender real-time protection enabled
description: Microsoft Defender Antivirus real-time protection should be enabled when Defender is present. Applicability: Windows Server 2019/2022/2025 benchmark scope (build 17763+). CIS Controls v8 (Control 10.1 IG1) requires deploying and maintaining anti-malware software.

references.name: CIS Microsoft Windows Server 2019/2022/2025 Benchmarks
Get-MpComputerStatus reference
CIS Controls v8, Control 10.1 (IG1): Deploy and Maintain Anti-Malware Software

remediation.description: Enable Defender real-time monitoring.
remediation.scriptTemplate: Set-MpPreference -DisableRealtimeMonitoring $false

considerations: Microsoft Defender real-time protection on Exchange servers must be configured with correct Exchange-specific exclusions to avoid false positives and performance degradation. Enabling real-time protection without the correct exclusions can cause database corruption or transport stalls. Verify all Microsoft-recommended exclusions are in place before enabling.

title: PowerShell Script Block Logging enabled
description: PowerShell Script Block Logging should be enabled to improve detection and auditability of script activity. Applicability: Windows Server 2019/2022/2025 benchmark scope (build 17763+). CIS (Section 18.9.104.1.2 L1) requires 'Turn on PowerShell Script Block Logging' to be set to 'Enabled'.

references.name: CIS Microsoft Windows Server 2019 Benchmark v3.0.0
PowerShell Group Policy settings
CIS Microsoft Windows Server 2022 Benchmark v3.0.0, Section 18.9.104.1.2 (L1): Ensure 'Turn on PowerShell Script Block Logging' is set to 'Enabled'

remediation.description: Enable Script Block Logging via PowerShell policy registry.
remediation.scriptTemplate: New-Item -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\ScriptBlockLogging' -Force | Out-Null; Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\ScriptBlockLogging' -Name EnableScriptBlockLogging -Type DWord -Value 1

considerations: Enabling PowerShell Script Block Logging generates detailed logs of all executed script blocks in the Security and Windows PowerShell event logs. This can produce high log volume on active systems. Ensure log retention and collection capacity are adequate before enabling on production Exchange servers.

title: RDP requires Network Level Authentication
description: Remote Desktop should require Network Level Authentication (NLA). Applicability: Windows Server 2019/2022/2025 benchmark scope (build 17763+). CIS (Section 18.10.28.3.8 L1) requires 'Require user authentication for remote connections by using Network Level Authentication' to be set to 'Enabled'.

references.name: CIS Microsoft Windows Server 2019/2022/2025 Benchmarks
NLA for Remote Desktop
CIS Microsoft Windows Server 2022 Benchmark v3.0.0, Section 18.10.28.3.8 (L1): Ensure 'Require user authentication for remote connections by using Network Level Authentication' is set to 'Enabled'

remediation.description: Require NLA for RDP-Tcp endpoint.
remediation.scriptTemplate: Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Terminal Server\WinStations\RDP-Tcp' -Name UserAuthentication -Type DWord -Value 1

considerations: Enabling Network Level Authentication (NLA) for RDP requires that connecting clients support CredSSP authentication. Very old RDP clients (pre-Vista) may not connect. Some RDP proxy solutions may require configuration updates. Test admin connectivity after enabling NLA before applying broadly.

title: WDigest UseLogonCredential disabled
description: WDigest should not store clear-text credentials in LSASS. Applicability: Windows Server 2019/2022/2025 benchmark scope (build 17763+). CIS (Section 18.3.7 L1) requires 'MSS: (UseLogonCredential) WDigest Authentication' to be set to 'Disabled'.

references.name: CIS Microsoft Windows Server 2019/2022/2025 Benchmarks
UseLogonCredential security guidance
CIS Microsoft Windows Server 2022 Benchmark v3.0.0, Section 18.3.7 (L1): Ensure 'MSS: (UseLogonCredential) WDigest Authentication' (UseLogonCredential registry value) is set to 'Disabled'

remediation.description: Disable WDigest credential caching by setting UseLogonCredential to 0.
remediation.scriptTemplate: New-Item -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\WDigest' -Force | Out-Null; Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\WDigest' -Name UseLogonCredential -Type DWord -Value 0

considerations: Disabling WDigest UseLogonCredential prevents cleartext password caching in LSASS memory, which is a common credential harvesting technique. There is no operational impact on Exchange functionality from disabling this setting. A reboot may be required for the change to take full effect.

title: IIS HSTS configuration baseline for Exchange sites
description: Validate IIS HSTS posture on Exchange-related websites, including unsupported usage on Exchange Back End, redirectHttpToHttps risk, and minimum max-age guidance. CISA (AA21-062A) recommends enabling HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) on Exchange web virtual directories.

references.name: CSS HSTS guidance
CISA AA21-062A: Mitigate Microsoft Exchange Server Vulnerabilities — enable HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) on Exchange web virtual directories

remediation.description: Review and correct IIS HSTS configuration for Exchange websites, especially Exchange Back End and redirectHttpToHttps settings.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: adjust HSTS settings in IIS and validate Exchange Back End compatibility.

considerations: Enabling HSTS instructs browsers to only connect to Exchange sites over HTTPS. If any IIS HTTP binding is required for any reason (e.g., certificate renewal challenges), HSTS will prevent HTTP connections for the duration of the max-age period. Test all Exchange virtual directories for HTTPS functionality before enabling HSTS.

title: Hybrid send connector TLS integrity
description: Validate hybrid/EXO send connector TLS settings (TlsAuthLevel, RequireTLS, TlsCertificateName, and TlsDomain) to reduce mail flow risk.

references.name: CSS Hybrid Connector checks

remediation.description: Correct hybrid send connector TLS settings and certificate bindings to match Exchange hybrid best practices.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: update send connector TlsAuthLevel/RequireTLS/TlsCertificateName/TlsDomain per hybrid design.

considerations: Hybrid send connector TLS settings affect mail routing to Exchange Online. Weakening TLS requirements may allow plaintext transport to Exchange Online. Verify the connector certificate is trusted by Exchange Online before changing TLS settings. Test hybrid mail flow after any connector certificate changes.

title: TLS insecure renegotiation values hardened
description: Ensure SCHANNEL insecure renegotiation registry values for clients and servers are set to secure baseline values.

references.name: CSS TLS hardening checks

remediation.description: Configure TLS versions consistently across Exchange servers. Set Client and Server Enabled and DisabledByDefault DWORD values under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\SCHANNEL\Protocols for each TLS version. Also configure SystemDefaultTlsVersions and SchUseStrongCrypto in .NET Framework registry paths. TLS 1.2 must be enabled; TLS 1.3 must be disabled for Exchange.
remediation.scriptTemplate: Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\SCHANNEL' -Name AllowInsecureRenegoClients -Type DWord -Value 0; Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\SCHANNEL' -Name AllowInsecureRenegoServers -Type DWord -Value 0

considerations: TLS renegotiation settings affect all TLS connections on the server at the Schannel layer. Disabling insecure renegotiation prevents certain downgrade attacks but may break connectivity with clients that require renegotiation for session resumption. Test with monitoring and SMTP partner systems before enforcing.

title: IIS loaded modules are signed and trusted
description: Detect unsigned or untrusted IIS modules that can represent security risk on Exchange hosts.

references.name: CSS IIS module anomaly checks

remediation.description: Remove or replace unsigned/untrusted IIS modules and validate module chain-of-trust.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: inventory IIS modules, remove unsigned modules, and validate trusted signatures.

considerations: Unsigned or untrusted IIS modules in Exchange virtual directories may have been installed by Exchange patches or third-party products. Removing a module required by Exchange or a legitimate third-party product will break the associated Exchange functionality. Verify the purpose of each flagged module before removal.

title: Server pending reboot cleared
description: No pending reboot indicators should be present in the registry (pending file rename operations, Windows Update pending reboot, or Component-Based Servicing pending keys). Pending reboots block Exchange cumulative update installation.

references.name: CSS reboot pending guidance

remediation.description: Reboot the server to clear pending reboot flags. If registry keys persist after the reboot, manually delete them from the indicated registry locations. Always back up the registry before making manual edits.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: plan and execute an approved reboot to clear pending reboot indicators.

considerations: Clearing a pending reboot clears only the state flag — the actual rebooted state is required for changes (such as software updates or service changes) to take effect. Schedule a maintenance window with DAG failover for the reboot. Verify all DAG members are in a healthy state before rebooting any member.

title: Dynamic memory disabled for virtualized Exchange hosts
description: Virtualized Exchange hosts should avoid dynamic memory allocation to maintain stable performance characteristics.

references.name: CSS dynamic memory check

remediation.description: Disable dynamic memory for Exchange virtual machines and align reserved/maximum memory with sizing guidance.
remediation.scriptTemplate: # Manual remediation required: disable dynamic memory in hypervisor configuration for Exchange VMs.

considerations: Dynamic memory for Exchange VMs creates unpredictable memory pressure that can cause Exchange process crashes and transport stalls. If dynamic memory is currently enabled, disabling it requires coordination with the virtualization team and a VM reboot. The VM should be sized with static memory according to Exchange sizing guidelines.

title: MSMQ feature not installed on Exchange hosts
description: MSMQ-related Windows features are generally not required for current Exchange baseline and should be removed when present.

references.name: CSS MSMQ feature check
